behaviors.description: The clause extractor identifies all key terms (parties, dates, obligations, penalties) present in the contract.
behaviors.justification: This behavior depends entirely on real LLM output. A unit test with a mocked LLM would always pass, hiding real failures like: the LLM missing certain clause types, poor extraction prompt formatting, response parsing breaking on unexpected formats, or API failures. The extractor must work with real model non-determinism—different contracts yield different extractions, and different model runs may miss edge cases.
behaviors.test_description: Call the real clause extractor with a diverse set of real or realistic contracts covering edge cases (multi-party agreements, unusual penalty structures, regulatory boilerplate). Verify that all major clause types present in ground-truth annotations are extracted. Run multiple times if using temperature > 0 to catch variance.

behaviors.description: The risk assessor flags language that would be problematic under the configured risk policy, even when the problematic phrasing is indirect or euphemistic.
behaviors.justification: Detecting subtle, euphemistic risk language is an LLM-native capability—it cannot be unit-tested because the risk detection depends on semantic understanding that only a real model possesses. Mocking would hide whether the LLM actually understands the configured policy, whether the prompt conveys policy nuance, and whether the model can catch semantic variations. A test must run against real contracts with real LLMs to catch failures like: the model missing indirect language, misclassifying benign language, or misinterpreting the risk policy.
behaviors.test_description: Provide the real risk assessor with a curated set of contracts containing known problematic clauses (both direct and euphemistic) plus benign clauses. Verify that the majority of problematic instances are flagged. Include adversarial test cases: identical risky terms worded differently, idiomatic expressions that mean 'high risk' but don't use standard terminology.

behaviors.description: The compliance checker correctly determines whether the contract meets all applicable regulatory requirements for the configured jurisdiction.
behaviors.justification: Regulatory compliance assessment depends on the LLM's knowledge of jurisdiction-specific requirements and its ability to apply them correctly. Unit tests with mocked LLM responses would hide critical failures: the LLM doesn't know regulations for a specific jurisdiction, the LLM misinterprets contract terms against those regulations, or the jurisdiction configuration isn't passed correctly to the LLM. Real failures occur when a model trained on limited regulatory data misses jurisdiction-specific obligations.
behaviors.test_description: Call the compliance checker with real contracts and specific jurisdiction configurations (e.g., GDPR for EU, state-specific contract law). Include edge cases: contracts that meet some but not all requirements, regulations that changed or are ambiguous. Verify compliance determinations against known ground-truth compliance labels. Test multiple jurisdictions to catch knowledge gaps.

behaviors.description: When two reviewers flag the same clause with conflicting assessments (e.g., extractor says 'standard' but risk assessor says 'high risk'), the merge step preserves both assessments with attribution.
behaviors.justification: The merge logic is deterministic—given three structured reviewer outputs, it should produce a deterministic combined report. Mock reviewer outputs are sufficient to test whether the merge correctly identifies conflicts, preserves both assessments, and maintains attribution. Unit tests would catch real failures: merge logic that silently picks one assessment over another, that loses attribution metadata, or that fails on conflict detection. The merge step's behavior is independent of LLM output quality.
behaviors.test_description: Create mock outputs from three reviewers that include at least one conflicting assessment on the same clause. Pass these mocks to the merge function. Verify that the output includes both assessments with clear attribution (e.g., 'Clause X: Extractor=standard, Risk Assessor=high risk'). Test edge cases: all three agree, all three disagree, partial overlap.

behaviors.description: The full pipeline — dispatching to all three reviewers, collecting results, merging into a unified report — completes correctly for a real contract without losing any individual reviewer's findings.
behaviors.justification: This is a multi-step agentic pipeline: dispatch to three parallel LLM reviewers (Agentic), collect results (System-level concurrency), merge (Unit logic). Real failures occur at the orchestration level: one reviewer times out and its findings are dropped, a race condition causes the result collector to miss one output, or the pipeline fails mid-way without returning partial results. Unit or Integration tests with mocked reviewers would not catch concurrency bugs, timeout handling, or the real LLM latency variance. System-level tests might catch some parallelization issues but miss individual LLM failures. Only a full Workflow test—running real reviewers, real parallelization, and real merge on real contracts—catches the end-to-end failure mode: 'we processed the contract but lost findings from one reviewer.'
behaviors.test_description: Run the full pipeline end-to-end with real contracts and real LLM calls to all three reviewers. Verify that the final merged report includes findings from all three reviewers. Include edge cases: one reviewer takes significantly longer (test timeout handling), malformed responses from one reviewer (test resilience), network glitches (test retry logic). Run multiple times with real contracts to catch non-deterministic losses.
